english_culture: In the US/UK, people prefer about an arm's length distance. Standing too close makes people uncomfortable.
spanish_culture: In Latin America/Spain, people stand closer and physical contact (hugs, cheek kisses) is normal.
tip: Don't stand too close to Americans/British people - they need more personal space than you're used to!
example_awkward: Standing very close to a coworker while talking
example_correct: Maintaining arm's length distance in professional settings

english_culture: Direct eye contact is expected and shows you're listening, honest, and engaged. Avoiding eye contact is suspicious.
spanish_culture: Eye contact varies by context; too much eye contact can be seen as aggressive or disrespectful depending on social hierarchy.
tip: Make steady eye contact when someone speaks to you - it shows respect and attention.
example_awkward: Looking down or away when someone is talking to you
example_correct: Looking directly at the speaker's eyes while they speak

english_culture: Being on time is very important. Arriving late is disrespectful and unprofessional. 'On time' means exactly on time or a few minutes early.
spanish_culture: Time is more flexible. Being 15-30 minutes late to social events is acceptable. Business meetings should be closer to on time, but delays are understood.
tip: Arrive 5-10 minutes early for important meetings or appointments. Americans/British people take time very seriously.
example_awkward: Arriving 20 minutes late to a business meeting without explanation
example_correct: Arriving 10 minutes early and apologizing if you're 2 minutes late

english_culture: Small talk is essential before business. English speakers discuss weather, sports, weekends, and local news to build rapport.
spanish_culture: While small talk exists, people may jump more quickly into personal relationships or business topics. Less emphasis on weather discussions.
tip: Start conversations with comments about weather or weekends. This is expected and helps create connection.
example_awkward: Immediately diving into business without any greeting or small talk
example_correct: Starting with 'How was your weekend?' before discussing work matters

english_culture: In the US, tipping 15-20% is expected for restaurants, taxis, barbers, and hotels. It's part of the service industry wage system. UK tips are usually 10-15%, Canada 15-20%.
spanish_culture: Tipping is less common and usually 5-10% is acceptable. In many countries, service is included in the bill. Tipping is appreciated but not expected.
tip: In the US, always tip 18-20% at restaurants. It's not optional - it's how servers get paid!
example_awkward: Not tipping or tipping less than 10% at a US restaurant
example_correct: Calculating 18-20% tip on top of the bill at an American restaurant

english_culture: Saying 'Merry Christmas' is common and acceptable. However, in diverse areas, 'Happy Holidays' is preferred to be inclusive of all religions and beliefs.
spanish_culture: 'Feliz Navidad' is the standard greeting. People are generally comfortable with Christmas greetings regardless of religion.
tip: Use 'Happy Holidays' in diverse/professional settings. 'Merry Christmas' is fine with people you know.
example_awkward: Saying 'Merry Christmas' to a group of people with diverse beliefs in a professional setting
example_correct: Saying 'Happy Holidays' in professional or diverse settings

english_culture: Many English-speaking companies are informal. Even executives may ask to be called by first names. Hierarchy exists but is often downplayed in daily interaction.
spanish_culture: Professional titles are important. Using titles (Señor/Señora, Doctor, Ingeniero) shows respect. First names with managers is less common and may seem disrespectful.
tip: When meeting someone, use their title until they tell you to use their first name. When in doubt, be more formal.
key_phrases: call me by my first name
example_awkward: Calling your boss 'John' when they haven't explicitly told you to
example_correct: Using 'Mr. Smith' until your boss says 'Please call me John'

english_culture: Business casual is common in many US offices. 'Casual Friday' allows jeans. Tech companies are very casual (jeans, t-shirts). Dress codes vary widely by industry.
spanish_culture: Business formal is more standard in many Spanish-speaking offices. Even in casual offices, people tend to dress more formally than equivalent US companies.
tip: Ask about dress code on your first day. In the US, people dress more casually than you might expect for office work.
example_awkward: Wearing very casual clothes (t-shirt with holes, athletic wear) to a professional office
example_correct: Wearing business casual - button-up shirt, nice pants, and dress shoes

english_culture: In business settings, gifts are minimal or avoided (conflict of interest concerns). Personal gifts should be modest. Gifts are opened immediately. Expensive gifts make people uncomfortable.
spanish_culture: Gifts are more common in business relationships. Opening gifts immediately is polite. Gifts can be more personal and meaningful.
tip: In the US, avoid expensive gifts at work. Keep it professional and modest. If someone gives you a gift, accept it gracefully.
example_awkward: Giving an expensive personal gift to your boss or a new business contact
example_correct: Giving a modest gift like chocolate or a pen to coworkers during holidays

english_culture: In the US, if you suggest dinner, you might pay. Among friends, splitting the bill equally ('going Dutch') is common. Business meals: the person who invited pays. Doggy bags are normal.
spanish_culture: The inviter typically pays. Splitting is less common. Taking leftovers home is becoming more common but less standard than in the US.
tip: Ask 'Should we split?' or check how it's done before assuming. In professional settings, the senior person usually pays.
example_awkward: Expecting the other person to pay when you suggested the restaurant
example_correct: Offering to pay or asking to split the bill explicitly before eating

topic: How Are You? (Not a Real Question)
english_culture: 'How are you?' is a greeting, not a genuine question. The expected answer is 'Good, how are you?' or 'Fine, thanks.' People don't give detailed health updates.
spanish_culture: '¿Cómo estás?' can be a genuine question expecting a more detailed answer about your actual well-being.
tip: When someone asks 'How are you?', just say 'Good, thanks, and you?' Don't give a long explanation of your actual problems.
example_awkward: Responding to 'How are you?' with a 10-minute story about your medical problems
example_correct: Responding 'Good, thanks! How are you?' and moving on to conversation

english_culture: Please and thank you are used constantly and expected. Forgetting them sounds rude. Saying 'thank you' for small favors is standard, even if you know the person well.
spanish_culture: Politeness is important, but 'please' and 'thank you' are used less frequently in informal situations with family and close friends.
tip: Say 'please' and 'thank you' often - it shows respect and is always appropriate in English.
example_awkward: Not saying 'thank you' when a coworker helps you with something
example_correct: Saying 'Thank you so much for your help!' to anyone who assists you

english_culture: Sarcasm and irony are common, especially in British and Australian English. Self-deprecating humor is appreciated. Dark humor is used in many settings.
spanish_culture: Humor tends to be more direct and less sarcastic. Sarcasm can be misunderstood or seem rude. Self-deprecating humor is less common.
tip: Be careful with sarcasm until you know people well - it's easy to misunderstand in English. Self-deprecating jokes are usually safe and liked.
example_awkward: Using heavy sarcasm with someone who doesn't know you well and having them take it literally
example_correct: Using light humor and following it up with 'I'm just kidding' to clarify

english_culture: Direct communication is valued. People appreciate straightforward feedback and honest opinions. Being vague or overly indirect is seen as confusing or dishonest.
spanish_culture: Communication can be more indirect to preserve relationships and avoid confrontation. Direct criticism can be seen as harsh or aggressive.
tip: Be direct and clear about what you want or mean. English speakers prefer honesty over politeness. Don't be afraid to state your opinion.
example_awkward: Saying 'Maybe we could think about it' when you mean 'No'
example_correct: Saying 'I don't think that will work because...' with clear reasons

english_culture: Plans are usually specific with exact times and locations. 'Let's hang out sometime' without specifics is considered vague and not a real plan. Following up with details is expected.
spanish_culture: Plans can be more flexible and spontaneous. Saying 'We should hang out sometime' is normal and may lead to spontaneous meetings. Time is less rigid.
tip: When making plans, confirm: the exact date, time, and location. 'Let's do coffee Friday at 3pm at the downtown Starbucks.' Vague plans often don't happen.
example_awkward: Saying 'Let's grab lunch sometime' without setting a specific time or day
example_correct: Saying 'Let's grab lunch on Thursday at noon at that Italian place downtown'

english_culture: English speakers, especially British people, tend to be quieter in public. Being loud in restaurants, buses, or trains is considered rude and inconsiderate.
spanish_culture: Public spaces tend to be louder. Conversations are often more animated and energetic. A higher noise level is normal and expected.
tip: Keep your voice down in public places like restaurants, buses, and libraries. Loud talking will get you stares.
example_awkward: Having a loud, animated phone conversation in a quiet train
example_correct: Speaking quietly and keeping conversations at a low volume in public transit

english_culture: Queuing in an orderly line is very important. Cutting in line is considered extremely rude. People queue for buses, shops, and waiting areas.
spanish_culture: Lines can be less organized. People may crowd or push gently to move forward. Queuing is expected but less strictly enforced in some places.
tip: Always wait in line and never cut in front. This is a serious breach of etiquette in English-speaking countries.
example_awkward: Walking directly to the front of a line to be served first
example_correct: Going to the back of the line and waiting your turn patiently

english_culture: British people apologize frequently, even when not at fault ('Sorry, could you pass the salt?'). Americans apologize less but still say sorry for mistakes. Apologies should be genuine.
spanish_culture: Apologies are sincere and used when truly at fault. Excessive apologies for minor things can seem insincere or weak.
tip: British people apologize a lot - don't take it as over-dramatic. Americans and others are more strategic with apologies.
example_awkward: Never apologizing even when you make a clear mistake
example_correct: Saying 'I apologize for that mistake. Here's how I'll fix it' with a genuine tone

english_culture: Dating is casual and people go on dates with different people. First date often involves split bills. Commitment is discussed explicitly. Texting/messaging is primary communication.
spanish_culture: Dating can be more family-oriented and traditional. The man often pays for dates. Relationships move toward commitment more quickly. Spending time with family is important early on.
tip: Be clear about what you want - casual dating or a relationship. Don't assume someone wants to be exclusive. Texting is normal dating communication.
example_awkward: Assuming someone wants to be in an exclusive relationship after one date
example_correct: Having a conversation after a few dates: 'Where do you see this going?'

english_culture: Work-life balance is increasingly important but implementation varies. Americans work long hours (often without paid vacation). UK has more vacation days. Checking emails after hours is common.
spanish_culture: Many Spanish-speaking countries have stronger legal protections for time off. Longer lunch breaks and siesta traditions exist. Clear separation between work and personal time.
tip: In the US, expect to work longer hours with less vacation. Don't be surprised if people check emails at night. Set boundaries when possible.
example_awkward: Refusing to check emails or work on weekends when your workplace culture expects it
example_correct: Being flexible with your schedule but also advocating for reasonable working hours

english_culture: Compliments are common in casual conversation. When complimented, people usually say 'Thank you!' directly. Deflecting compliments seems awkward or rude.
spanish_culture: Compliments are appreciated but modest deflection is more common. Saying 'Gracias' but then diminishing the compliment is normal humility.
tip: Accept compliments gracefully by saying 'Thank you!' Just say thank you - don't argue with the compliment or deny it.
example_awkward: When complimented on your presentation, saying 'Oh, it was nothing' or 'I don't think it was that good'
example_correct: When complimented, simply saying 'Thank you! I really appreciate that'

english_culture: Customer service is expected to be friendly and helpful. The customer is often 'right.' Tipping service workers is expected. Service workers are expected to maintain positive attitudes.
spanish_culture: Customer service varies widely. The relationship is more transactional. Friendliness depends on the individual worker. Less emphasis on 'customer is always right'.
tip: Be nice to service workers - they're doing their job. Say 'please' and 'thank you' and they'll be more helpful. Tipping ensures good service.
example_awkward: Being rude or demanding to a service worker without saying 'please' or 'thank you'
example_correct: Politely asking for help with a smile and thanking the worker for their assistance
